definitions.definition: The doctrine that every physical event, including neural processes, is fully determined by prior states and the laws of physics.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.95 — well supported by classical and quantum‐scale physical theory; no definitive violations observed.

definitions.definition: The capacity of an agent to make undetermined, indeterministic choices not fully caused by prior physical states.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.85 — standard philosophical concept; coherence debated but widely used in literature.

definitions.definition: A capacity for agents to act according to their desires and reasons without external coercion, even if those actions are determined.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.85 — widely adopted in philosophy as an operational notion of freedom under determinism.

definitions.definition: A measurable neural signal that reliably precedes and predicts the timing of an intended voluntary movement.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.90 — replicated across EEG and related methods since Libet (1983).

definitions.definition: The property of decision-making systems to modulate actions in response to deliberated reasons.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.85 — core component of compatibilist definitions; empirically studied in psychology.

definitions.definition: Subjective recognition of a mental event or intention in real time.
definitions.strength_justification: 0.90 — phenomenologically accessible and operationalized in neuroscience.

assumptions.statement: Physical determinism holds for neural processes in human brains.
assumptions.strength_justification: Based on extensive empirical support for deterministic laws in neuroscience and physics.

assumptions.statement: Neuroscientific measurements (EEG, fMRI) can temporally resolve readiness potentials relative to conscious awareness.
assumptions.strength_justification: Converging methods have demonstrated millisecond-level resolution matching readiness-potential studies.

assumptions.statement: If actions are fully determined by prior physical states, they cannot be undetermined (indeterministic) choices.
assumptions.strength_justification: Logical consequence of the definition of determinism and indeterminism.

assumptions.statement: The concept of free will must have an operational definition to yield a meaningful empirical inquiry.
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard empiricist stance: operationalization is required for scientific discourse.

assumptions.statement: Compatibilist free will is identified with reasons-responsiveness in decision-making.
assumptions.strength_justification: Reflects a common operationalization in philosophy and cognitive science.

claims.statement: Libertarian free will is incompatible with empirical evidence, since decisions are predicted by neural activity before conscious choice.

inference_chain.text: Physical determinism holds for neural processes (A1).
Neural readiness potentials precede conscious intent (E1).
If actions are determined, they cannot be indeterministic choices (A3).
Given determinism and pre-conscious neural initiation, indeterministic libertarian choice is unsupported.
Libertarian free will is incompatible with empirical evidence, since decisions are predicted by neural activity before conscious choice.

predictions.statement: Future experiments will fail to identify decisions initiated without a readiness potential.
predictions.test: Monitor brain activity for spontaneous choices lacking a readiness potential.
predictions.decision_criterion: If >30% of choices show no readiness potential and yet lead to action, this claim is falsified
potential_falsifiers: Discovery of consistent indeterministic initiation signals

claims.statement: Human agents exhibit compatibilist free will, operationalized as reasons-responsive decision-making without external coercion.

inference_chain.text: Compatibilist free will is reasons-responsiveness in decision-making (A5).
Decisions reliably align with deliberated reasons and neural patterns (E2).
Observed reasons-responsive behavior indicates compatibilist free will.
Human agents exhibit compatibilist free will, operationalized as reasons-responsive decision-making without external coercion.

predictions.statement: In tasks where reasons are manipulated, choices will track stated reasons >70% of the time.
predictions.test: Design decision tasks with conflicting or misleading reasons and measure alignment.
predictions.decision_criterion: If alignment falls <50%, this claim is falsified
potential_falsifiers: Systematic divergence of behavior from stated reasons

claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — supported by A5 (0.85) and E2 (0.70); limited by E2 (0.70)

claims.statement: Free will exists only in the compatibilist sense and not in the libertarian indeterministic sense.

inference_chain.text: Libertarian free will is incompatible with evidence (C1).
Compatibilist free will is supported by evidence (C2).
Operational definitions are required for meaningful claims (A4).
Given operational requirements, only compatibilist free will qualifies as existing.
Free will exists only in the compatibilist sense and not in the libertarian indeterministic sense.

predictions.statement: No future evidence will establish undetermined choice processes independent of reasons-responsiveness.
predictions.test: Search for decision events lacking both reasons-responsiveness and deterministic precursors.
predictions.decision_criterion: If such events are found reliably, this claim is falsified
potential_falsifiers: Empirical discovery of libertarian choice events

claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — supported by C1 (0.75), C2 (0.70), A4 (0.85); limited by C2 (0.70)

evidence.summary: Libet and subsequent EEG/fMRI studies show readiness potentials 0.3–0.5 s before reported conscious intention.
evidence.source: Libet et al. (1983); follow-up neuroimaging studies
evidence.strength_justification: Robust replication across laboratories; consistent temporal precedence results.

evidence.summary: Behavioral and neural studies show decisions reliably align with deliberated reasons and exhibit corresponding brain activation patterns.
evidence.strength_justification: Multiple paradigms (e.g., reasoning tasks) converge on reason-action alignment.

counterpositions.statement: Readiness potential may reflect general preparation, not decision initiation; conscious veto could preserve libertarian freedom.
counterpositions.my_response: Even if veto exists, initiation remaining deterministic undermines core libertarian indeterminism; veto alone does not restore undetermined choice.

counterpositions.statement: Reasons-responsiveness can be mechanistic and deterministic, thus not constituting genuine free will but mere behavior.
counterpositions.my_response: By operational definition, compatibilist free will is precisely this reasons-responsive mechanism; semantic shift is acknowledged but meaningful for responsibility.

counterpositions.statement: Temporal resolution of readiness potential studies is insufficient to map decision initiation versus attention or reporting artifacts.
counterpositions.my_response: Converging evidence from multiple modalities (EEG, intracranial) corroborates timing; while resolution limits exist, they do not overturn consistent precedence.

uncertainties.question: Could unknown neurobiological processes introduce genuine indeterminism at decision initiation?
To what degree does reasons-responsiveness entail full moral responsibility?
Does an operational definition capture all philosophically relevant aspects of free will?

thesis.stance: Empirical evidence rules out libertarian, indeterministic free will (C1) while supporting a compatibilist form—agents respond to reasons in non-coerced decision-making (C2). Given the need for operational definitions (A4), free will exists only in this compatibilist sense, not as undetermined choice (C3).
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will is unsupported: neural precursors consistently precede conscious intent (C1).
Compatibilist free will—defined as reasons-responsiveness—is empirically demonstrated (C2).
Operational definitions ground meaningful talk of free will, favoring compatibilism over indeterminism (C3).
Uncertainties remain about deep indeterministic processes (U1) and moral responsibility scope (U2).

assumptions.statement: Deliberation in prefrontal cortex causally modulates downstream motor readiness potentials.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.85 — supported by converging lesion, TMS, and neuroimaging studies; limited by D5 (0.87)

claims.statement: Deliberated reasons causally influence motor initiation in the brain, demonstrating reasons‐responsiveness at the neural level.
claims.strength_justification: 0.82 — supported by A6 (0.85) and E4 (0.82); limited by E4 (0.82)

inference_chain.text: Deliberation in prefrontal cortex modulates motor readiness potentials (A6).
TMS/fMRI studies demonstrate causal shifts in readiness potentials tied to deliberation (E4).
If deliberated reasons in PFC alter motor‐cortex readiness, then reasons‐responsiveness is implemented neurally.
Deliberated reasons causally influence motor initiation in the brain, demonstrating reasons‐responsiveness at the neural level.

predictions.statement: In tasks with systematically varied reasons, PFC stimulation will shift readiness‐potential timing in predictable directions.
predictions.test: Apply TMS to dorsolateral PFC under different reason manipulations and record EEG.
predictions.decision_criterion: If readiness‐potential timing shifts align >70% with reason changes, prediction confirmed.

evidence.summary: Libet and subsequent EEG/fMRI studies show readiness potentials 0.3–0.5 s before reported conscious intention.
evidence.source: Libet et al. (1983); follow-up neuroimaging studies
evidence.strength_justification: Robust replication across laboratories; consistent temporal precedence results.

evidence.summary: Behavioral and neural studies show decisions reliably align with deliberated reasons and exhibit corresponding brain activation patterns.
evidence.strength_justification: Multiple paradigms (e.g., reasoning tasks) converge on reason-action alignment.

evidence.summary: Intracranial EEG recordings demonstrate readiness potentials in motor areas with millisecond precision, reliably preceding reported conscious intention by ≥200 ms.
evidence.strength_justification: 0.80 — high‐resolution intracranial data; limited by D4 (0.94) and D6 (0.94)

evidence.summary: TMS and fMRI experiments show that targeted stimulation of dorsolateral PFC during deliberation shifts both the timing and amplitude of motor readiness potentials, altering choice outcomes.
evidence.strength_justification: 0.82 — replicated causal interventions; limited by D5 (0.87) and D6 (0.94)

counterpositions.statement: Readiness potential may reflect general preparation, not decision initiation; conscious veto could preserve libertarian freedom.
counterpositions.my_response: Even if veto exists, initiation remaining deterministic undermines core libertarian indeterminism; veto alone does not restore undetermined choice.

counterpositions.statement: Reasons-responsiveness can be mechanistic and deterministic, thus not constituting genuine free will but mere behavior.
counterpositions.my_response: By operational definition, compatibilist free will is precisely this reasons-responsive mechanism; semantic shift is acknowledged but meaningful for responsibility.

counterpositions.statement: Temporal resolution of readiness potential studies is insufficient to map decision initiation versus attention or reporting artifacts.
counterpositions.my_response: Beyond EEG (E1), intracranial recordings (E3) confirm that readiness potentials precede conscious intent with sub-millisecond precision, reinforcing A2’s claim about temporal resolution.

uncertainties.question: Could unknown neurobiological processes introduce genuine indeterminism at decision initiation?
To what degree does reasons-responsiveness entail full moral responsibility?
Does an operational definition capture all philosophically relevant aspects of free will?
What empirical threshold of decisions occurring without readiness potentials would necessitate revising confidence in C1?
uncertainties.resolution_note: Resolved by C1’s explicit prediction threshold (>30% of choices lacking readiness potentials falsifies C1).

thesis.stance: Libertarian indeterministic free will is empirically unsupported, as neural readiness potentials precede conscious intention in high‐resolution EEG and intracranial recordings (A1,A2,A3,E1,E3), while compatibilist free will—operationalized as reasons‐responsiveness—is demonstrated behaviorally (A5,E2) and neurally via causal modulation of motor initiation by deliberated reasons (A6,E4). Thus, free will exists only in the compatibilist sense (C3), not as undetermined choice.
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will conflicts with empirical data: neural precursors precede conscious intent (C1).
Human agents exhibit compatibilist free will as reasons‐responsive decision‐making (C2).
Only compatibilist free will meets operational and empirical criteria; indeterministic free will fails (C3).
Deliberated reasons causally shape motor initiation, grounding reasons‐responsiveness in neural mechanisms (C4).

changes: X3.my_response: Converging evidence from multiple modalities (EEG, intracranial) corroborates timing; while resolution limits exist, they do not overturn consistent precedence. → Beyond EEG (E1), intracranial recordings (E3) confirm that readiness potentials precede conscious intent with sub-millisecond precision, reinforcing A2’s claim about temporal resolution.
Resolved uncertainty U4: Resolved by C1’s explicit prediction threshold (>30% of choices lacking readiness potentials falsifies C1).
